title: War Prediction, Control, And The Rebellion Of Imagination
subtitle: A February 15, 2026 interview that starts with Iran and widens into Pax Judaica, elite control, reading, AI, and love
voice_policy: Direct interview prose that keeps the host's pressure where it sharpens Jiang's argument. Preserve the dated force of Jiang's geopolitical and spiritual claims without flattening them into neutral recap.
trace_policy: Paragraph claims and selective marks carry exact transcript refs. High-speculation passages remain framed as Jiang's dated interpretations from the interview rather than independent reporting.
dek: The title promises Iran war prediction, but the interview's real shape is stranger. Jiang begins by explaining why he predicted conflict with Iran and why listeners project hidden patrons onto him. He then turns that opening into a larger diagnosis: America is split between old finance and new tech oligarchy, Iran would expose the weakness of an optics-first empire, Pax Judaica points toward AI surveillance, and the only durable answer to a control civilization is disciplined attention, imagination, and love.
thesis: This source matters because it shows how quickly Jiang moves from tactical forecast to total world model. A war with Iran is never just a military question. It becomes a pressure test for manufacturing, morale, logistics, religion, elite faction struggle, information control, and the coming AI order. The second half then reveals what the first half was really serving: a defense of the imagination against systems that want obedience, spectacle, and managed attention. By the end, true wealth is no longer money or empire. It is the human capacity to read, imagine, cooperate, and love under collapsing control structures.

source_notes.text: The interview is dated 2026-02-15. It preserves Jiang's predictions and interpretations as source-grounded claims from that date rather than updating them into present-tense reporting.

The public title foregrounds Iran, but the conversation ranges much further: elite faction conflict, Israel and end-times politics, religion, literature, AI, parenting, money, and a closing metaphysics of imagination and love. The read keeps that whole arc rather than forcing the interview into a single-topic war briefing.

Several later sections are deliberately speculative, especially around hidden networks, false flags, crypto-Jewish elite lineages, child-sacrifice ritual, Bitcoin origins, COVID planning, and Antarctica. The read keeps the strongest conceptual movement from those sections without presenting their most documentary-thin claims as settled fact.

opening.heading: Core Reading
opening.text: The interview begins with a reputation problem and ends with a metaphysics. Jiang says people think he must be fed elite information because he predicted the Iran war, talks fluently about hidden power, and rose too quickly. But he uses that opening to make a larger claim: the world is being reorganized through war pressure, tech oligarchy, AI surveillance, and managed fear. Iran matters because it would expose the logistical and moral weakness of an empire built for optics. Yet the final answer is not another state program or ideology. It is to reclaim attention, imagine new forms of life, and remember that love, not control, is what actually binds the world together.

marks.text: I sort of blew up last June
trying to create a Palantir police state
this war in Iran, it will be a disaster
the greatest act of rebellion that you can do in today's world is to reclaim your attention
love is the unifying force of the universe

beats.heading: Why The Prediction Made Him Look Managed
beats.summary: The host begins as an admirer, but the first substantive turn is about why Jiang's sudden visibility makes people suspect hidden patrons. Jiang answers that prediction itself produces conspiracy around the predictor.

paragraphs.text: The host arrives as a true initiate rather than a neutral interviewer. He has watched lecture after lecture, taken pages of notes, and wants Jiang's biography before his geopolitics. That matters because the interview never really behaves like a journalistic challenge session. It behaves like a long attempt to pull the hidden logic out of a thinker whose lectures already feel to the host like a map.

Jiang's first important answer is about why listeners think he must be connected to hidden power. He says his rise accelerated after he predicted conflict with Iran, and that people assume anyone who speaks fluently about intelligence, transnational capital, and evil must be receiving elite guidance. His answer is simple and revealing: prediction is mistaken for allegiance, and description is mistaken for endorsement. The early Pax Judaica material is his example. He says people hear the forecast and miss the moral revulsion built into it.

marks.text: I seem to know a lot about secret societies
Pax Judaica is a very evil thing

beats.heading: Old Deep State, New Deep State
beats.summary: A question about imperial doctrine becomes a map of elite faction struggle. Jiang says transnational finance and Silicon Valley are fighting over the American state, and the prize is a new AI control system.

paragraphs.text: Jiang's answer to Venezuela and empire is not that Washington has one center. He says the American crisis is now a civil war inside the ruling structure itself: finance against tech oligarchy, old deep state against new deep state. Palantir, digital ID, data centers, and AI infrastructure appear here not as neutral modernization but as the next command apparatus. The interview does not frame these as separate issues. Monetary instability, police power, crypto volatility, border spectacle, and surveillance all belong to one transition.

marks.text: the old deep state and new deep state
digital ID digital currency

paragraphs.text: What keeps the answer from sounding purely structural is that Jiang roots it in ordinary social cost. Empire means taxes spent on wars no one wants, migration shocks nobody can stabilize, and corruption so visible that he invokes his own 2006 time in Afghanistan. The security state created after 9/11 becomes the precedent for everything that follows: spectacular emergency on the surface, administrative expansion underneath. By the end of this section, war, surveillance, and social exhaustion already look like the same machine seen from different angles.

marks.text: that's a Palantir police state
the military industrial complex

beats.heading: Iran As The War That Exposes Optics Empire
beats.summary: Asked whether Iran could trigger world war or end-times conflict, Jiang answers with a logistics-and-belief model: America lacks capacity, Iran has prepared materially and spiritually, and television doctrine fails against a society willing to absorb sacrifice.

paragraphs.text: The interview's advertised subject finally arrives when the host asks whether Iran is the catalyst. Jiang's answer is not one dramatic battlefield prediction but a layered impossibility argument. America lacks manufacturing slack, political will, popular emotional investment, and a realistic sense of the terrain. Shock and awe can destroy, but it cannot govern Tehran. He says the fantasy of a quick decapitation campaign mistakes cinematic tempo for strategic time.

marks.text: America is not ready for a war
that's going to take years

paragraphs.text: Jiang then adds what the purely military explanation lacks: Iran's preparation is moral and civilizational as well as tactical. Drones and ballistic missiles are only the hardware layer. The deeper layer is a Shia tradition shaped by persecution, martyrdom, and group cohesion, fused in his telling with an older Persian eschatological pride. That is why American doctrine fails. It is built for the audience at home, for Desert Storm optics, for enemies expected to fold. Against a society that interprets sacrifice as honor, television war stops working.

marks.text: the greatest honor to sacrifice yourself for the cause
it's all for the audience back at home

beats.heading: From Pax Judaica To Techno-Marxism
beats.summary: The middle stretch connects Israel, anti-Semitism, transnational capital, communism, and expert rule into one theory of historical engineering. The thread running through it is not consistency of documentary proof but consistency of control logic.

paragraphs.text: This section is one of the interview's most volatile, but its internal pattern is clear. Jiang treats anti-Semitism, Zionist migration pressure, central-bank integration, and imperial regime change as coordinated mechanisms rather than separate historical themes. The goal is to make populations portable, states governable, and resistance legible to capital. He folds countries without central-bank submission into the same target list as societies resisting Israel's regional dominance.

marks.text: the entire point of the military complex is to make the world safe for transnational capital

paragraphs.text: The sharper conceptual move comes when he connects Pax Judaica to data governance. Fear for children, Epstein-file disgust, border violence, and ideological panic become instruments for consenting to deeper technical management. That is why the conversation shifts from Zionism and Hitler to communism and then to Brzezinski: what matters is the recurring dialectical method. Each ism discredits one opponent, creates another dependency, and hands more interpretive authority to experts and systems. By the end Jiang names the latest version directly: techno-Marxism.

marks.text: the entire point of Pax Judaica is to create an AI surveillance state
techno-Marxism

beats.heading: Iran As The Last Block And The War Trigger
beats.summary: The skipped middle arc makes the interview's Iran theory more specific. Jiang says Iran is the last barrier to Israeli regional supremacy, that an unwinnable war is useful because it weakens America while destabilizing Iran, and that the likely trigger would be an overseas false-flag event rather than a homeland spectacle.

paragraphs.text: Jiang next turns his end-times frame into a strategic model. He says Iran matters because it is the last blocking power preventing Israel from becoming the uncontested force in the Middle East, and he argues that some of the relevant actors do not need America to win a war there. In his telling, an unwinnable conflict is useful precisely because it shatters American capacity, removes Washington from the region, and leaves a weakened Iran behind. He presents that sequence as prediction rather than documentary proof, but it is one of the interview's clearest bridges between eschatology and war planning.

marks.text: Iran is the last coming block
you make this war unwinnable

paragraphs.text: The prediction sharpens when the host asks for the next trigger. Jiang says prior attempts to break Iran cheaply through decapitation strikes, currency pressure, unrest, and covert networks failed because the regime proved more resilient than expected. From there he offers a dated forecast, not an established fact: the remaining path to war is a false flag that hits American forces somewhere in the Middle East, followed by a blame cascade aimed at Iran. He then folds Saudi Arabia and the GCC into the same map. Their public distance from Israel is, in his model, theater designed to keep their oil infrastructure from becoming the obvious target if Iran retaliates economically.

marks.text: we can expect a false flag operation
much more likely to happen overseas
It's trying to play the victim

beats.heading: What The Demonic World Is Trying To Do
beats.summary: The interview's longest stretch turns from political theology to moral anthropology. Jiang's recurring distinction is not simply religion versus unbelief, but manipulation versus directness, memorable evil versus remembered good, and control versus empathy.

paragraphs.text: Much of the later theological material is highly contestable in content, but the moral architecture is consistent. Jiang repeatedly describes a world where empire rewards masks, layered identities, manipulation, and strategic impurity. That is why, in his reading, directness can be politically weak and why traditions adapt under pressure. Whether he is talking about chosenness, Frankism, or organized religion, the recurring concern is always the same: what kind of soul survives in a world built by maneuver and domination?

marks.text: this is a world of Satan
if you're too simple, you're too direct, that limits your maneuverability in politics

paragraphs.text: The strongest part of this long stretch comes when the host asks why Jiang chooses good over evil. Jiang answers with free will, insomnia, memory, and empathy. Evil is not glamorous liberation. It is entrapment: once someone serves the demonic, he says, that person must keep serving it in order to live with himself. The rival ideal is not purity but remembered beneficence. To be memorable like Epstein is one path. To be remembered for helping others toward the good is another. Reading, bullying, alienation, and perspective become the schooling that made empathy possible.

marks.text: it's not about living a good life, it's living a memorable life
once you embrace the demonic, then you are trapped

beats.heading: Attention, Reading, AI, And The Secret Of Love
beats.summary: The closing movement gathers literature, scripture, money, crypto, AI, COVID, and civilizational collapse into one final claim: what survives control is the imagination disciplined by reading, community, and love.

paragraphs.text: The interview's most reusable public claim arrives through literature. Jiang says the basic rebellion available to ordinary people is to reclaim attention. Reading is not hobbyism here. It is anti-colonization. A good book becomes a mirror, a portal into the divine, and training in individuality. From there he widens the argument: schools teach process because process is administratively convenient, but actual creativity comes through imagination, inspiration, and possession by meaning rather than by algorithm.

marks.text: reclaim your attention
a portal into the divine
it's all through imagination

paragraphs.text: The final synthesis is almost a counter-creed to the whole surveillance arc. True wealth is not money but imagination, influence, knowledge, and the ability to cooperate when systems fail. In a blackout, strangers direct traffic before authority arrives; in collapse, neighbors improvise before institutions do. That is why AI appears at the end as Antichrist rather than merely as software: it is the totalization of dependence. COVID becomes the rehearsal model for that dependence, while Jiang's own antidote is stubbornly small-scale and human. His children keep him sane. His view of Plato softens because he now values imagination more. The grand secret, he says, is simple enough to sound embarrassing after two hours of empire and demons: love unifies the universe, and imagination animates it.

marks.text: what true wealth is, is our imagination
the imagination will carry you through
it is AI. It's literally AI
My children

questions.question: Is Iran the likely catalyst for World War III or the end times?
questions.answer: Jiang says a war with Iran would be disastrous for the United States because America lacks manufacturing depth, political will, terrain realism, and public conviction, while Iran has both material preparation and civilizational resolve.

questions.question: What is the distinction between Shia and Sunni martyrdom here, and why does it matter for Iran?
questions.answer: Jiang says the Shia historical experience of persecution produced cohesion and an honor code around sacrifice, and he ties that to an older Persian eschatological identity that makes Iran unusually difficult to break through fear alone.

questions.question: Why would parents still accept mass-surveillance tools if they are already learning that elite systems prey on children?
questions.answer: Jiang says fear for children is exactly the conversion mechanism: elite depravity creates panic, panic creates consent, and consent opens the door to digital ID, tracking, and AI-administered security.

questions.question: Why do you choose good over evil if the demonic seems to offer power?
questions.answer: Jiang says free will makes both paths possible, but evil becomes a trap that destroys sleep, conscience, and empathy, while remembered good can inspire other people toward the light.

questions.question: Where should wealth be stored if fiat, Bitcoin, and empire all look unstable?
questions.answer: Jiang says true wealth is not primarily money but imagination, knowledge, influence, and the cooperative capacity people discover when systems fail and neighbors have to improvise together.

questions.question: Do you believe the Antichrist will be AI-generated?
questions.answer: Jiang says yes: AI is the endpoint of a surveillance civilization that wants total dependence, and he connects it directly to the wider Pax Judaica control model.
